coredata.dc:title: Naphthalimide-based multifunctional AIEgens: Selective, fast, and wash-free fluorescence tracking and identification of Gram-positive bacteria
coredata.dc:creator: Sayed, Sayed Mir
Xu, Ke-Fei
Jia, Hao-Ran
Yin, Fei-Fei
Ma, Liang
Zhang, Xiaodong
Khan, Arshad
Ma, Qian
Wu, Fu-Gen
Lu, Xiaolin
coredata.dc:description: Pathogenic infections, particularly caused by Gram-positive bacteria (G+), pose a serious threat to human health, and therefore the fast and accurate discrimination of G+ bacteria from Gram-negative bacteria (G–) and fungi is highly desirable. Organic molecules with facile synthesis, robust photostability, good biocompatibility, and high selectivity toward pathogens are urgently needed in the clinical diagnosis and therapy. To this end, herein we report the synthesis of two naphthalimide-based bioprobes named tetraphenylethylene-naphthalimide (TPE-NIM) and triphenylamine-naphthalimide (TPA-NIM) with aggregation-induced emission (AIE) characteristic. First, the staining capacity of the designed AIEgens toward six kinds of bacteria and two kinds of fungi was evaluated. Both TPE-NIM and TPA-NIM showed a high degree of binding/imaging selectivity for G+ bacteria over G− bacteria and fungi via a wash-free protocol. Second, the two AIEgens had the ability to visualize the biofilms formed by G+ bacteria (Staphylococcus aureus) and can quickly track the G+ bacteria (Staphylococcus aureus) in red blood cell suspensions. Third, we have revealed that electrostatic attraction and hydrophobic interaction both contribute to the selective binding of the AIEgens toward G+ bacteria. In view of the high binding/imaging specificity toward G+ bacteria, low hemolysis rates, and low toxicity toward the bacterial cells, these AIEgens can be applied for the clinical detection of pathogenic infections caused by G+ bacteria and broaden the theranostic applications of AIE materials.
coredata.dcterms:subject: wash-free bacterial imaging
Gram-type differentiation
Biofilm staining
AIE probe
Bacterial distinguishment

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Binding abilities of TPA-NIM and TPE-NIM toward pathogens
Tracking of G+ bacteria in red blood cell (RBC) suspensions
Staining mechanism of TPA-NIM and TPE-NIM toward G+ bacteria

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency: National Natural Science Foundation of China

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (A and B) Absorption and fluorescence (FL) emission spectrum (Ex = 430 nm) of TPA-NIM in DCM. (C) Normalized fluorescence (FL) emission spectra of TPA-NIM dissolved in hexane, toluene, THF, and acetone (Ex = 430 nm). (D and E) Absorption and fluorescence (FL) emission spectrum (Ex = 378 nm) of TPE-NIM in chloroform. (F) Normalized fluorescence (FL) emission spectra of TPE-NIM dissolved in hexane, toluene, THF, and acetone (Ex = 378 nm).

(A) Fluorescence (FL) emission spectra of TPA-NIM in the DMSO/H2O mixtures with different water fractions (based on volume). Ex = 430 nm. (B) Plot of the changes in the FL intensities of TPA-NIM with different water fractions. The data were from the maximal FL intensities of the curves in (A). (C) Fluorescence (FL) emission spectra of TPE-NIM in the DMSO/H2O mixtures with different water fractions (based on volume). Ex = 378 nm. (D) Plot of the changes in the FL intensities of TPE-NIM with different water fractions. The data were from the maximal FL intensities of the curves in (C). The concentration of TPA-NIM and TPE-NIM was 0.1 mM.

Confocal images of G+ bacteria (S. aureus, B. subtilis, and M. luteus; left: A−I) and G− bacteria (E. coli, P. aeruginosa, and P. vulgaris; right: J−R) after treatment with TPA-NIM (10 μg/mL) for 15 min.

Confocal images of G+ bacteria (S. aureus, B. subtilis, and M. luteus; left: A−I) and G− bacteria (E. coli, P. aeruginosa, and P. vulgaris; right: J−R) after treatment with TPE-NIM (10 μg/mL) for 15 min.
Selective staining of S. aureus in the mixtures of E. coli and S. aureus by TPA-NIM (A–C) and TPE-NIM (D–F). Both concentrations of TPA-NIM and TPE-NIM were 10 μg/mL.
Growth curves of S. aureus bacteria after treatment with different concentrations of (A) TPA-NIM or (B) TPE-NIM.
Three dimension-reconstructed confocal images for the S. aureus biofilms that received the treatment of (A) TPA-NIM or (B) TPE-NIM. Both concentrations of TPA-NIM and TPE-NIM were 20 μg/mL.
Confocal images illustrating the selective staining of S. aureus cells in RBC suspensions via (A–C) TPA-NIM and (D–F) TPE-NIM.
Zeta potential values of S. aureus and E. coli before (control) and after being treated with the indicated probes.
Confocal fluorescence images of S. aureus cells receiving various treatments.

Synthetic routes of TPE-NIM and TPA-NIM. Reagents and conditions: (i) Zn, TiCl4, tetrahydrofuran (THF), 0–25 °C, 12 h. (ii) n-Butyllithium solution, trimethyl borate, THF, −78 °C, 3 h (iii) C2H5OH, reflux, 2 h. (iv) Pd(PPh3)4, Na2CO3, mixed solvent (toluene: ethanol: water), 90 °C, 12 h. (v) Pd(PPh3)4, Na2CO3, mixed solvent (toluene: ethanol: water), 90 °C, 12 h.
Molecular structures of AIEgens and a general representation of their adsorption onto the surfaces of G+ bacteria. Note that the AIEgens and the bacteria are not drawn in real scales.

head.ce:title: Naphthalimide-based multifunctional AIEgens: Selective, fast, and wash-free fluorescence tracking and identification of Gram-positive bacteria

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: State Key Laboratory of Bioelectronics, School of Biological Science and Medical Engineering, Southeast University, 2 Sipailou Road, Nanjing, 210096, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Biological Science and Medical Engineering

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: State Key Laboratory of Bioelectronics, School of Biological Science and Medical Engineering, Southeast University, 2 Sipailou Road, Nanjing, 210096, P. R. China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Jiangsu Key Laboratory of Oral Diseases, Nanjing Medical University, Department of General Dentistry, Affiliated Hospital of Stomatology, Nanjing Medical University, Nanjing, 210029, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Jiangsu Key Laboratory of Oral Diseases

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Jiangsu Key Laboratory of Oral Diseases, Nanjing Medical University, Department of General Dentistry, Affiliated Hospital of Stomatology, Nanjing Medical University, Nanjing, 210029, P. R. China

ce:footnote.ce:note-para: S.M.S. and K.F.X. contributed equally to this manuscript.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Pathogenic infections, particularly caused by Gram-positive bacteria (G+), pose a serious threat to human health, and therefore the fast and accurate discrimination of G+ bacteria from Gram-negative bacteria (G–) and fungi is highly desirable. Organic molecules with facile synthesis, robust photostability, good biocompatibility, and high selectivity toward pathogens are urgently needed in the clinical diagnosis and therapy. To this end, herein we report the synthesis of two naphthalimide-based bioprobes named tetraphenylethylene-naphthalimide (TPE-NIM) and triphenylamine-naphthalimide (TPA-NIM) with aggregation-induced emission (AIE) characteristic. First, the staining capacity of the designed AIEgens toward six kinds of bacteria and two kinds of fungi was evaluated. Both TPE-NIM and TPA-NIM showed a high degree of binding/imaging selectivity for G+ bacteria over G− bacteria and fungi via a wash-free protocol. Second, the two AIEgens had the ability to visualize the biofilms formed by G+ bacteria (Staphylococcus aureus) and can quickly track the G+ bacteria (Staphylococcus aureus) in red blood cell suspensions. Third, we have revealed that electrostatic attraction and hydrophobic interaction both contribute to the selective binding of the AIEgens toward G+ bacteria. In view of the high binding/imaging specificity toward G+ bacteria, low hemolysis rates, and low toxicity toward the bacterial cells, these AIEgens can be applied for the clinical detection of pathogenic infections caused by G+ bacteria and broaden the theranostic applications of AIE materials.

The schematic shows our developed AIEgens for selective, fast, and wash-free fluorescence tracking and identification of Gram-Positive Bacteria.
Image 1

ce:section.ce:para: Bacteria and fungi, the two important members of the microbe’s family, on one hand play key roles in human life by maintaining the ecological equilibrium, purifying the living environment, and boosting the immune system, but on the other hand can cause terrible infections, which is a major cause of the human diseases worldwide [1–3]. Indeed pathogenic infections caused by bacteria and fungi pose a serious threat to the safe use of food, air, and water, resulting in the public health issues and environmental pollution [4]. One common strategy adopted by pathogens for their survival in a host system is the formation of an amorphous and dynamic structure called biofilm, which is not only resistant to the host immune system but also shows resistance to antibiotics, leading to persistent infections [5], which may cause fatal diseases and expensive medical costs [6]. On the basis of the cell surface components, pathogenic bacteria have been classified into two broad classes named Gram-positive (G+) and Gram-negative (G−), after the discovery of the Gram-staining method in 1884 [6,7].

G+ bacteria, a serious threat to the mankind that can affect millions of lives every year [8], are responsible for various nosocomial infections [9]. At the same time, some G+ bacteria can increase the chemo-resistance of the tumor tissues toward the chemodrugs, which decreases the effectiveness of the chemodrugs to suppress tumor growth and metastasis [10]. In addition, owing to the excessive use of antibiotics, bacteria are now becoming more resistant toward antibiotics, which is an alarming situation for human health [11]. For example, as per the World Health Organization (WHO) report, methicillin-resistant Staphylococcus aureus (S. aureus), a kind of G+ bacteria, shows resistance toward many kinds of antibiotics and is becoming a deadly threat to human being [2,12,13] by causing urinary tract infections, pneumonia, septicemia, and pericarditis, which could be lethal if not properly treated [14]. Considering the threat posed by G+ bacteria, their fast discrimination and identification from other pathogens are highly demanded. In this regard, various approaches have been developed, among which the Gram-staining method is a standard and popular diagnostic one to differentiate G+ bacteria from G− bacteria. But, its procedure is complicated and false positive results can be generated [15,16]. Other methods, such as quantitative real-time polymerase chain reaction (PCR) [17], standard plate counting [18], Raman spectroscopy [19], and immunological techniques [20] have also been developed and achieved success in the pathogen discrimination and identification. However, these techniques are generally time-, energy-, and cost-consuming, and also require sophisticated instruments [10], which substantially limit their clinical applications, especially in the developing countries. Therefore, in order to achieve effective medical treatments and reduce the medical costs, facile, fast, cost-effective, and reliable diagnostic techniques for pathogen infections are urgently required.

Recently, fluorescence imaging technologies with advantages like easy operation, high selectivity and sensitivity, excellent brightness, photostability, and real time monitoring of the complicated biological processes have attracted worldwide attention in biomedical science [21,22]. However, conventional fluorescent probes, such as rhodamine, fluorescein, BODIPY, and cyanine suffer from the aggregation-caused quenching effect and seriously undergo photobleaching during the long-term imaging, which greatly limit their practical clinical applications [22]. Inorganic nanoparticles, such as quantum dots usually have high brightness and are photostable, but their potential toxicity caused by their heavy metal contents is always a main concern when used for biomedical purposes [22–30]. An alternative bioprobe family, AIE luminogens, usually possessing high photostability and low cytotoxicity, can be considered as an ideal candidate to overcome these drawbacks [10]. AIEgens generally have no or weak fluorescence in the solution states but are strongly fluorescent in aggregate states owing to the restriction of intramolecular motions [31]. The concept of AIEgens was coined in 2001 [32], since then AIEgens have found multiple applications in the imaging of the mammalian cells and pathogens [2,4,10,33–48]. Normally, most of the fluorescence-based methods to image pathogens need a washing step to remove the unbound dyes to reduce the background noises that often result in the loss of pathogens, which on one hand decreases the accuracy of the experiment and on the other hand leads to an extra experimental step [49,50]. Therefore it would be fascinating to discover more bioprobes with AIE characteristics for wash-free biomedical imaging applications.

Herein, we report the design and synthesis of two naphthalimide-based AIEgens named TPE-NIM and TPA-NIM for the selective discrimination of live pathogens via a wash-free protocol. TPE-NIM and TPA-NIM can selectively stain G+ bacteria, even in the mixture of G− bacteria and Fungi via wash-free and fast (15 min) staining procedures. The designed AIEgens can also track the G+ S. aureus cells in red blood cell (RBC) suspensions without causing notable hemolysis, and can successfully visualize the biofilms formed by S. aureus cells. Moreover, the binding mechanism of the AIEgens toward G+ bacteria is also carefully studied, and the results clearly reveal that both electrostatic and hydrophobic interactions contribute to this binding process.

TPA-NIM and TPE-NIM were designed and synthesized as per the routes given in Scheme 1 and characterized by the nuclear magnetic resonance (NMR) and mass spectroscopy techniques (Figs. S1–S7). The naphthalimide core was chosen as an electron-withdrawing unit. It was further decorated with tertiary amine as the biologically active component of the designed AIEgens and electron-donating units (TPA and TPE) to enhance the π−π conjugation capability and endow the products with the AIE property. The tertiary amine with alkaline nature may strongly interact with the lipoteichoic acid and teichuronic acid present in the cell wall of G+ bacteria via electrostatic interaction and can play a key role in the selective staining of the pathogens [2].

Photophysical properties of the designed AIEgens were studied via ultraviolet–visible (UV–vis) and fluorescence spectroscopic methods. UV–vis and fluorescence spectra of TPA-NIM and TPE-NIM were recorded in dichloromethane (DCM) and chloroform, respectively. As illustrated in Fig. 1 , TPA-NIM exhibits the absorption maximum at 430 nm (Fig. 1A) and emission maximum at 594 nm (Fig. 1B), and TPE-NIM exhibits the absorption maximum at 378 nm (Fig. 1D) and emission maximum at 537 nm (Fig. 1E). The Stokes shifts for TPA-NIM and TPE-NIM were found to be ∼164 nm and ∼159 nm, respectively, and such large Stokes shifts are highly desirable for biomedical applications to avoid the self-quenching in the aggregated states [2]. The effect of the solvent polarity on the emission wavelength of the as-prepared AIEgens was also studied. Four solvents, hexane, toluene, tetrahydrofuran (THF), and acetone with the polarity indices of 0.1, 3.1, 4.0, and 5.1, respectively, were chosen. With an increase in the polarity from hexane to acetone, a bathochromic/red shift with decrease in the fluorescence intensity was observed for both AIEgens (Fig. 1C and F, and Fig. S8), indicating the transformation of the AIEgens into the twisted intramolecular charge transfer state [51,52].

We further evaluated the AIE behaviors of the designed AIEgens. For TPA-NIM, mixtures of dimethyl sulfoxide (DMSO)/water were used. In pure DMSO, TPA-NIM did not exhibit any fluorescence. Upon the addition of water, the fluorescence was enhanced and the intensity reached the maximum at 50% water fraction (based on volume), which then sharply decreased upon the further addition of water (Fig. 2 A and B). The AIE behavior of TPE-NIM was also studied in dimethyl sulfoxide (DMSO)/water system. In pure DMSO, TPE-NIM exhibited weak orange fluorescence with a maximum emission wavelength of 575 nm, which was blue-shifted (to a maximum emission wavelength of 507 nm) upon the addition of water and reached the maximum intensity at 70% water fraction (based on volume), which then gradually decreased upon the further addition of water (Fig. 2C and D). The decrease in the fluorescence intensity after 50% water fraction in the case of TPA-NIM and 70% water fraction in the case of TPE-NIM could be attributed to the change in the size of the nanoaggregates, as reported previously [53,54]. The size of the nanoaggregates formed at different water contents were measured via the dynamic light scattering (DLS) technique. In the case of TPA-NIM, it was found that the average size of the nanoaggregates decreased from 583 ± 135 nm at 50% water fraction to 71.6 ± 45.4 nm at 70% water fraction (Fig. S9), which may have caused the quenching of the fluorescence. Similarly, in the case of TPE-NIM, the size of the nanoaggregates decreased from 51.2 ± 15.6 nm at 70% water fraction to 27.2 ± 6.8 nm at 90% water fraction (Fig. S10), which may be responsible for the decline in the fluorescence intensity. Besides, we have also confirmed the excellent stability of the AIE aggregates formed by TPA-NIM and TPE-NIM in water (Fig. S11). On the basis of the above optical studies, we can thus conclude that TPA-NIM and TPE-NIM are typical AIEgens with the twisted intramolecular charge transfer (TICT) characteristic.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Binding abilities of TPA-NIM and TPE-NIM toward pathogens
ce:section.ce:para: To study the binding efficiencies of the designed AIEgens toward pathogens, initially two different kinds of bacteria, S. aureus (G+ bacteria) and E. coli (G− bacteria) were chosen. S. aureus and E. coli bacteria were separately incubated with TPA-NIM or TPE-NIM at 37 °C for 15 min, and the staining performance of the AIEgens toward the two types of bacteria was checked via confocal microscopy. The confocal imaging results, which are given in Fig. 3 (A−C and J−L) and Fig. 4 (A−C and J−L), clearly showed that TPA-NIM and TPE-NIM lit up S. aureus, while no fluorescence signals were observed for E. coli. These results encouraged us to further explore the staining abilities of these AIEgens toward other microbes, such as fungi. For this purpose, yeast was chosen and incubated with these AIEgens for 15 min, no fluorescence signals were observed, as shown in Fig. S12. These results supported the imaging selectivity of the designed AIEgens toward G+ bacteria.

To further confirm the imaging selectivity of the AIEgens toward G+ bacteria, we examined the capability of these AIEgens to stain two other kinds of G+ bacteria (B. subtilis and M. luteus) and G− bacteria (P. aeruginosa and P. vulgaris) and one kind of fungi (T. reesei). As shown in Fig. 3 (D−I and M−R) and 4 (D−I and M−R), bright fluorescence signals were observed only for the G+ bacteria (B. subtilis and M. luteus), while no fluorescence signals were observed for G− bacteria (P. aeruginosa and P. vulgaris). Similarly, no fluorescence signals were observed for fungi (Figs. S12C, D, G, and H).

In addition, we performed the staining experiments for the mixed G+ bacteria (S. aureus) and G− bacteria (E. coli) (1:1). The bacterial mixture was incubated separately with TPA-NIM and TPE-NIM for 15 min, and the staining results of the AIEgens were observed via confocal microscopy. One can see from Fig. 5 that these AIEgens selectively stained G+ bacteria, while no fluorescence was observed for G− bacteria. Similar result was also observed for a mixed microbial system containing G+ bacteria (S. aureus), G− bacteria (E. coli), and fungi (T. reesei). Again, one can see from Fig. S13 that the designed AIEgens successfully targeted and stained S. aureus, while no fluorescence was observed for E. coli and T. reesei. These results further supported that TPA-NIM and TPE-NIM have strong staining affinity toward G+ bacteria.

Next, S. aureus was used as a model strain to investigate the bacterial detection sensitivity of TPA-NIM and TPE-NIM. It was found that TPA-NIM and TPE-NIM were responsive starting from 103 CFU/mL and the fluorescence intensity increased sharply above 106 CFU/mL bacterial concentration, as shown in Fig. S14. The calculated detection limits for TPA-NIM and TPE-NIM are 1.8 × 103 CFU/mL and 3.9 × 103 CFU/mL, respectively. The detection sensitivities of TPA-NIM and TPE-NIM are higher than or equivalent to the previously reported AIEgen-based bacterial sensors [49,55–58]. We have also compared the staining performance of the designed AIEgens with that of a commercially available probe, wheat germ agglutinin−Alexa Fluor 488 conjugate (WGA), and it was found that TPA-NIM and TPE-NIM have better staining performance toward G+ bacteria than that of WGA (see Fig. S15).

An ideal fluorescent probe suitable for the imaging of live bacteria should avert severe cytotoxicity toward the bacterial specimens. To evaluate the cytocompatibility of TPA-NIM and TPE-NIM, we monitored the concentration- and time-dependent toxicities of the two probes against S. aureus. As shown in Fig. 6 A, the growth curves of the bacteria treated with increasing concentrations (10–100 μg/mL) of TPA-NIM displayed a similar trend to that of non-treated bacteria, suggesting the negligible cytotoxicity of this fluorescent probe. Likewise, TPE-NIM (Fig. 6B) scarcely exhibited an evident inhibition effect on the bacterial growth at the concentrations of 0–60 μg/mL, despite a slight decrease of cell viabilities at 100 μg/mL. Given that the concentration of the two probes used for fluorescence imaging was only 10 μg/mL, we reason that they are eligible for live bacterial tracking and observations.

Biofilms, which act as a cell reservoir and help the bacterial colonies to repopulate are responsible for many persistent infections and chronic wounds [59]. The first step to successfully treat these biofilm-caused infections is the early detection of biofilms. Although the RNA/DNA-based high-throughput detection and biofilm-specific antibodies and antigens are commonly used for the detection of biofilms [5], these approaches are either expensive or time-consuming, and also need complicated procedures. Therefore, cheap, fast, and simple detection methods are required for clinicians to devise a proper therapeutic strategy for treating the biofilm-induced infections. In this regard, fluorescence imaging can be a suitable alternative to the above-mentioned diagnostic methods. Taking advantage of the high sensitivity and selectivity of our AIEgens (TPA-NIM and TPE-NIM) toward G+ bacteria, it is important to check the capacity of our AIEgens to visualize the biofilms formed by S. aureus cells. The results in Fig. 7 clearly indicated that both probes could successfully stain the S. aureus biofilms. In contrast, the two probes failed to stain the biofilms formed by E. coli cells as shown in Fig. S16.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Tracking of G+ bacteria in red blood cell (RBC) suspensions
ce:section.ce:para: Blood infections caused by pathogenic bacteria are the main causes of sepsis, which result in the high mortality and morbidity rates in humans [60]. The inability to rapidly detect and identify pathogens in the early stages of infections is still a pivotal health issue [61]. However, the conventional techniques used for the pathogens detection and recognition, such as blood culture, PCR, etc. [61], are either time-consuming or insensitive to detect bacteria at low concentrations in a blood sample [62–65]. In addition, these methods also require expensive equipments and complex procedures for the detection and identification of the target pathogens [61,66]. Therefore, a facile, fast, cost-effective, and reliable diagnostic technique that can realize the discrimination of the pathogens in blood samples is urgently needed. It is hence necessary to examine if TPA-NIM and TPE-NIM can be used to detect G+ bacteria in a suspension containing both G+ bacteria and RBCs. The designed AIEgens with relatively low hemolysis rates (Fig. S17) were applied to trace the G+ bacteria (S. aureus cells) in RBC suspensions and the results are shown in Fig. 8 . The RBC suspensions were infected with S. aureus cells, and then treated with TPA-NIM and TPE-NIM. After 15 min of incubation, both probes successfully targeted S. aureus cells in the RBC suspensions without causing notable damage to the RBCs.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Staining mechanism of TPA-NIM and TPE-NIM toward G+ bacteria
ce:section.ce:para: According to Fig. 1 (C and F) and Fig. 2, TPA-NIM and TPE-NIM are nonfluorescent in water with the photoluminescence quantum yields of 1% and 3%, respectively, but have significantly enhanced fluorescence in less polar solvents (and water/organic solvent mixtures) with the photoluminescence quantum yields of 39.4% and 36.7% in chloroform, respectively. The wash-free fluorescence imaging performance of the planktonic G+ bacteria and G+ bacteria in biofilms (Fig. 7) suggested that the two probes existed in a less polar, aggregated form within the bacterial cells, which ensured their stronger fluorescence signal intensities than those from the background.

As is well known, G+ bacteria, G− bacteria, and fungi are distinctly different in their surface components. Our AIEgens, TPA-NIM and TPE-NIM, have tertiary amine groups (Scheme 2 ) that should have electrostatic interaction with the acidic components (lipoteichoic acid and teichuronic acid) present in the cell walls of G+ bacteria, which may be responsible for the selective staining of G+ bacteria. In contrast, G− bacteria and fungi are lack of such components (Scheme S1), and thus the two probes failed to stain these microbes. To confirm the electrostatic attraction between the two probes and the cell surfaces of G+ bacteria, we conducted zeta potential analysis. The results in Fig. 9 revealed that the negative surface charges of S. aureus were partially neutralized in the presence of TPA-NIM or TPE-NIM, suggesting the successful binding of the probe molecules to the bacterial surfaces. As control groups, the zeta potential values of E. coli showed no significant change before and after the addition of these probes. Beside, our designed AIEgens are hydrophobic in nature, and hence the hydrophobic interaction between the AIEgens and bacteria cannot be excluded. To confirm the hydrophobic interaction of AIEgens with G+ bacteria, Triton X-100 was applied to disrupt the hydrophobic interaction between the AIEgens and G+ bacteria. S. aureus was chosen as the model to confirm the role of the hydrophobic interaction in the selective staining of G+ bacteria. The results in Fig. 10 showed that after the treatment by Triton X-100, the S. aureus cells stained by TPA-NIM or TPE-NIM had weaker fluorescence signals compared to the corresponding control groups. This confirms the positive effect of the hydrophobic interaction between AIEgens and S. aureus on the staining process. Additionally, to confirm that Triton X-100 can only destroy the hydrophobic interaction between the AIEgens and S. aureus cells rather than the cell wall of S. aureus, wheat germ agglutinin−Alexa Fluor 488 conjugate (the whole conjugate was hereafter abbreviated as WGA) was used to stain the cell wall of S. aureus. The confocal imaging results in Fig. 10 clearly show that there was no significant difference between the control group and Triton X-100-treated group after WGA staining, proving that the cell wall of S. aureus was intact after Triton X-100 treatment. On the basis of the above experimental observations, it can be concluded that both electrostatic attraction and hydrophobic interaction play key roles in the selective staining of G+ bacteria.

In this research, we report two new AIEgens named TPA-NIM and TPE-NIM developed by ourselves. In these AIEgens, the naphthalimide core was chosen as an electron-accepting unit, which was then functionalized with a tertiary amine for biological applications, and further decorated with triphenylamine and tetraphenylethylene as electron-donating moieties for extended conjugation and AIE property. It was found that the two AIEgens can selectively stain G+ bacteria but cannot stain other microbes like G− and fungi. We have also confirmed that TPA-NIM and TPE-NIM can successfully stain and image the biofilms formed by G+ bacteria (S. aureus cells) and selectively visualize G+ bacteria (S. aureus cells) in RBC suspensions. Staining mechanism of AIEgens toward G+ bacteria was also studied. It was found that both electrostatic attraction and hydrophobic interaction play key roles in the selective staining of G+ bacteria. We believe that the two AIEgens with wash-free imaging property, low hemolysis rates, and negligible toxicity to the bacterial cells can find practical applications in Gram-type differentiation and efficient selection of suitable antibiotics for infection treatments in the very near future.

Benzophenone and 4-bromophenyl(phenyl)methanone were purchased from Adamas-beta (Shanghai, China). TiCl4, zinc powder, n-butyllithium solution (2.5 M in hexane), 3-dimethylaminoprpylamine, Triton X-100, and 4-(diphenylamino)phenylboronic acid (TPA-B(OH)2) were obtained from Aladdin Co. Ltd. (Shanghai, China). 4-Bromo-1,8-naphthalic anhydride was obtained from Energy Chemical (Shanghai, China). Tetrakis(triphenylphosphine)palldium(0) (Pd(PPh3)4) and trimethyl borate were purchased from Mackline (Shanghai, China). Tetrahyrdrofuran (THF) was dried with CaH2, and other reagents were of analytical grade and used as received. Millipore filtration system (Millipore, Billerica, MA) was used for water purification. The chemical structures of the intermediates and final products were characterized by NMR spectroscopy (via a Bruker DRX-400 MHz spectrometer) in CDCl3/DMSO‑d 6 at room temperature. High resolution mass spectrometry (HRMS) was performed on an Agilent 6230 Accurate-Mass Time-of-Flight (TOF) LC/MS system. Photophysical properties were studied on a UV–vis spectrophotometer (UV-2600, Shimadzu, Japan) and a spectrofluorophotometer (RF-5301PC, Shimadzu, Japan). Confocal images were recorded on a confocal laser scanning microscope (TCS SP8, Leica, Germany). Sizes of the nanoaggregates were measured via a zetasizer (Nano ZS, Malvern Instruments, UK). Wheat germ agglutinin−Alexa Fluor 488 conjugate (WGA) was purchased from Invitrogen (Carlsbad, CA).

1.0 g (3.61 mmol) 4-bromo-1,8-naphthalic anhydride was added with 30.0 mL absolute ethanol and heated to 80 °C. To this boiling suspension, 0.41 g (3.97 mmol) 3-dimethylaminoprpylamine was injected dropwise and refluxed for further 2 h. The resulting solution was hot filtered, cooled to room temperature, and then an equal volume of deionized water was added to the cooled solution, stirred for a while at room temperature that resulted in the formation of a thick precipitate, which was vacuum filtered, washed with water, and finally dried at 60 °C under vacuum overnight to give compound DAP-NIM as a light brown solid (yield 88%). 1H NMR (400 MHz, CDCl3): δ(ppm) = 1.94 (m, 2H), 2.28 (s, 6H), 2.47 (m, 2H), 4.23 (t, J = 8.0 Hz, 2H), 7.85 (m, 1H), 8.05 (d, J = 8Hz, 1H), 8.42 (d, J = 8Hz, 1H), 8.58 (m, 1H), 8.66 (m, 1H).

0.2 g (0.554 mmol) DAP-NIM, 0.176 g (0.610 mmol) 4-(diphenylamino)phenylboronic acid (TPA-B(OH))2, 0.016 g (0.014 mmol) (Pd(PPh3)4), and sodium carbonate 0.176 (1.66 mmol) were taken into a schlenk flask and degassed multiple times. After that, 14.0 mL toluene, 4.0 mL absolute ethanol, and 2.0 mL deionized water were injected into the schlenk flask, respectively. The system was then refluxed under nitrogen at 90 °C for 12 h. After the reaction completion as per the thin-layer chromatography, the mixture was cooled to the room temperature and extracted with chloroform. The organic phase was dried over anhydrous MgSO4, filtered, and concentrated under the reduced pressure. The crude product was purified via column chromatography using the 1:1 mixture of ethyl acetate and methanol as eluent. The obtained product was dried under vacuum overnight to give TPA-NIM as a light orange solid (yield: 88%). 1H NMR (400 MHz, CDCl3): δ(ppm) = 1.91 (m, 2H), 2.26 (s, 6H), 2.46 (m, 2H), 4.19 (m, 2H), 7.02 (m, 2H), 7.13 (m, 6H), 7.25 (m, 4H), 7.30 (m, 2H), 7.65 (t, J = 8.0 Hz, 2H), 8.34 (m, 1H), 8.56 (m, 2H). 13C NMR (100 MHz, CDCl3): δ(ppm) = 25.81, 38.67, 45.08, 57.12, 121.23, 122.51, 122.88, 123.64, 125.05, 126.67, 127.69, 128.89, 129.49, 130.05, 130.80, 130.96, 131.19, 131.93, 132.87, 146.82, 147.35, 148.38, 164.19, 164.39. HRMS (ESI): (m/z) Calcd for C35H32N3O2 + [M+H]+ 526.2490, found 526.2488.

TPE-NIM was synthesized as per the procedures for TPA-NIM (except that 0.176 g of TPA-B(OH)2 was replaced by 0.230 g of TPE-B(OH)2; the detailed synthetic processes of 1-(4-bromophenyl)-1,2,2-triphenylethylene (TPE-Br) and 4-(1,2,2-triphenylvinyl) phenylboronic acid (TPE-B(OH)2) can be found in the previous literature [67] and the obtained product was dried under vacuum overnight to give TPE-NIM as a yellow solid (yield: 85%). 1H NMR (400 MHz, CDCl3): δ(ppm) = 1.96 (m, 2H), 2.28 (s, 6H), 2.47 (t, J = 8.0 Hz, 2H), 4.28 (m, 2H), 7.11 (m, 3H), 7.15 (m, 6H), 7.19 (m, 6H), 7.24 (m, 2H), 7.28 (m, 2H), 7.70 (m, 2H), 8.21 (d, J = 8Hz, 1H), 8.64 (m, 2H). 13C NMR (100 MHz, CDCl3): δ(ppm) = 26.16, 38.85, 45.44, 57.35, 121.63, 122.91, 126.66, 126.72, 127.73, 127.78. 127.85, 128.72, 129.26, 130.00, 130.78, 131.12, 131.30, 131.38, 131.42, 131.57, 132.63, 136.69, 140.22, 141.96, 143.36, 143.41, 143.60, 144.18, 146.72, 164.14, 164.33. HRMS (ESI): (m/z) Calcd for C43H37N2O2 + [M+H]+ 613.2850, found 613.2841.

Sayed Mir Sayed: Writing - original draft, contributed to this work, contributed to the design and synthesis of the AIEgens, wrote the original manuscript. Ke-Fei Xu: contributed to this work, contributed to the biological staining and imaging work. Hao-Ran Jia: Writing - original draft, contributed to this work, revised the manuscript first, revised the manuscript. Fei-Fei Yin: Writing - original draft, contributed to this work, revised the manuscript. Liang Ma: Writing - original draft, contributed to this work, revised the manuscript. Xiaodong Zhang: Writing - original draft, contributed to this work, revised the manuscript. Arshad Khan: Writing - original draft, contributed to this work, revised the manuscript. Qian Ma: Writing - original draft, contributed to this work, revised the manuscript. Fu-Gen Wu: Writing - original draft, contributed to this work, revised the manuscript. Xiaolin Lu: Writing - review & editing, Writing - original draft, contributed to this work, reviewed and revised the manuscript.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by the Southeast University Postdoctoral Innovation Talent Funding Program (3207032004A4), the National Natural Science Foundation of China (81801029 and 21673037) and the Fundamental Research Funds for the Central Universities (3207032006C3).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: The following is the Supplementary data to this article:

Other experimental descriptions, interaction modes between the AIEgens and bacteria/fungi, NMR spectra, high resolution mass spectra, DLS results, confocal images of fungi and bacteria, and toxicity and hemolysis evaluation results (PDF).
Multimedia component 1 Multimedia component 1

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.aca.2020.12.037.

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: M.A. Fischbach, C.T. Walsh, Antibiotics for emerging pathogens, Science, 325 (2009) 1089-1093.

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: R. Hu, F. Zhou, T. Zhou, J. Shen, Z. Wang, Z. Zhao, A. Qin, B.Z. Tang, Specific discrimination of Gram-positive bacteria and direct visualization of its infection towards mammalian cells by a DPAN-based AIEgen, Biomaterials, 187 (2018) 47-54.

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: G.J. Liu, S.N. Tian, C.Y. Li, G.W. Xing, L. Zhou, Aggregation-induced-emission materials with different electric charges as an artificial tongue: Design, construction, and assembly with various pathogenic bacteria for effective bacterial imaging and discrimination, ACS Appl. Mater. Interfaces, 9 (2017) 28331-28338.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Electrospun fibrous mats with conjugated tetraphenylethylene and mannose for sensitive turn-on fluorescent sensing of Escherichia coli

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Polyion complex micellar nanoparticles for integrated fluorometric detection and bacteria inhibition in aqueous media

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Y. Li, X. Hu, S. Tian, Y. Li, G. Zhang, G. Zhang, S. Liu, Polyion complex micellar nanoparticles for integrated fluorometric detection and bacteria inhibition in aqueous media, Biomaterials, 35 (2014) 1618-1626.

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: C. Yang, F. Hu, X. Zhang, C. Ren, F. Huang, J. Liu, Y. Zhang, L. Yang, Y. Gao, B. Liu, J. Liu, Combating bacterial infection by in situ self-assembly of AIEgen-peptide conjugate, Biomaterials, 244 (2020) 119972.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Detection of bacterial alkaline phosphatase activity by enzymatic in situ self-assembly of the AIEgen-peptide conjugate

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: X. Zhang, C. Ren, F. Hu, Y. Gao, Z. Wang, H. Li, J. Liu, B. Liu, C. Yang, Detection of bacterial alkaline phosphatase activity by enzymatic in situ self-assembly of the AIEgen-peptide conjugate, Anal. Chem., 92 (2020) 5185-5190.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Biofilm delays wound healing: a review of the evidence

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: D.G. Metcalf, P.G. Bowler, Biofilm delays wound healing: A review of the evidence, Burns Trauma, 1 (2013) 5-12.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Aggregation-induced emission-based ionic liquids for bacterial killing, imaging, cell labeling, and bacterial detection in blood cells

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: J. Shi, M. Wang, Z. Sun, Y. Liu, J. Guo, H. Mao, F. Yan, Aggregation-induced emission-based ionic liquids for bacterial killing, imaging, cell labeling, and bacterial detection in blood cells, Acta Biomater., 97 (2019) 247-259.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Rapid detection of single bacteria in unprocessed blood using integrated comprehensive droplet digital detection

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: D. Kang, M.M. Ali, K. Zhang, S.S. Huang, E. Peterson, M.A. Digman, E. Gratton, W. Zhao, Rapid detection of single bacteria in unprocessed blood using integrated comprehensive droplet digital detection, Nat. Commun., 5 (2014) 5427.

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: N. Zaraee, F.E. Kanik, A.M. Bhuiya, E.S. Gong, M.T. Geib, N.L. Unlu, A.Y. Ozkumur, J.R. Dupuis, M.S. Unlu, Highly sensitive and label-free digital detection of whole cell E. coli with interferometric reflectance imaging, Biosens. Bioelectron., 162 (2020) 112258.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Pathogen detection: a perspective of traditional methods and biosensors

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: O. Lazcka, F.J. Del Campo, F.X. Munoz, Pathogen detection: A perspective of traditional methods and biosensors, Biosens. Bioelectron., 22 (2007) 1205-1217.

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: P. Rajapaksha, A. Elbourne, S. Gangadoo, R. Brown, D. Cozzolino, J. Chapman, A review of methods for the detection of pathogenic microorganisms, Analyst, 144 (2019) 396-411.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Development of nanoparticle-based optical sensors for pathogenic bacterial detection

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Luminogenic materials constructed from tetraphenylethene building blocks: synthesis, aggregation-induced emission, two-photon absorption, light refraction, and explosive detection

Chem., 22 (2012) 232-240.
